description: MITRE ATT&CK rules for network traffic analysis. These rules analyze the 15-feature CICIDS-subset flow vector extracted from tshark output (packet counts, byte volumes, rates, TCP flag counts, and directional ratio) to flag scanning, denial-of-service, C2 beaconing, and exfiltration patterns.

rules.description: Detects an elevated number of SYN packets combined with a high forward-packet count within a single flow window, consistent with reconnaissance scanning behavior where an attacker probes many ports or hosts with connection-initiation packets to map open services.
rules.recommendation: Identify the source host and enumerate every destination port/IP it attempted to reach in the surrounding time window. Check firewall/IDS logs for correlating scan alerts. Determine whether the scan originated from an internal host (possible lateral reconnaissance) or external address. Block the source at the perimeter if external, or isolate the host if internal.

rules.description: Detects an extreme volume of SYN packets in a single flow, well beyond what reconnaissance scanning would generate, consistent with a SYN flood attack attempting to exhaust connection-handling resources on the destination host.
rules.mitre_technique_name: Network Denial of Service: Direct Network Flood
rules.recommendation: Engage upstream/ISP-level or CDN-based DDoS mitigation for the targeted destination. Enable SYN cookies on the affected host or load balancer. Identify whether source addresses are spoofed (distributed flood) or concentrated (single-source flood) to choose the appropriate blocking strategy. Preserve flow logs for post-incident capacity planning and evidence.

rules.description: Detects an abnormally high count of RST (reset) flags within a flow, which can indicate a reset-based denial-of-service technique, aggressive port scanning that closes connections immediately, or an on-path attacker injecting resets to disrupt legitimate sessions.
rules.recommendation: Determine which side of the connection is generating the resets and whether they correlate with a specific application or service. Check for connection-based DoS patterns targeting a single destination port. Rule out a misbehaving legitimate client before escalating as malicious.

rules.description: Detects a long-lived flow with very low byte throughput, consistent with periodic command-and-control beaconing where an implant sends small heartbeat/check-in messages at intervals rather than transferring bulk data — a pattern designed to blend in with normal low-volume background traffic.
rules.recommendation: Extract the destination IP/domain and port and check it against threat intelligence feeds and passive DNS history. Look for the same destination recurring across multiple flows/hosts at regular time intervals (a strong beacon signature). Capture a full packet capture of the next occurrence for payload/protocol analysis. Correlate with the malware AI pipeline's findings for any C2 strings extracted from binaries on the source host.

rules.description: Detects a flow with a very high backward byte count, indicating a large volume of data was transferred from the monitored network segment out toward the remote host — consistent with staged data being exfiltrated over an established connection.
rules.recommendation: Identify the source host and destination endpoint immediately and determine what data was accessible to that host prior to the transfer. Preserve the flow record and, if possible, a packet capture for content analysis. Check DLP and file-access logs on the source host for the staged data. Block the destination and reset credentials/sessions for the affected host as a precaution.

rules.description: Detects a flow with a very high PSH (push) flag count, indicating the sending side is rapidly pushing data segments to the application layer rather than allowing normal buffering — a pattern consistent with bulk data transfer bursts often seen during exfiltration or large payload delivery.
rules.recommendation: Correlate this flow with byte-volume features (Flow Bytes/s, Total Length of Bwd/Fwd Packets) to confirm whether it represents genuine bulk transfer. Identify the application/process on the source host responsible for the connection. Rule out legitimate high-throughput services (backups, streaming) before treating as suspicious.

rules.description: Detects a flow with an extreme down/up packet ratio, where one side of the connection receives vastly more packets than it sends. Highly asymmetric flows can indicate exfiltration (client sends little, receives acknowledgments while pushing data on a separate reverse channel), reflection/amplification abuse, or one-sided beaconing.
rules.recommendation: Inspect which side of the connection is the amplifier versus the requester to determine if this is amplification abuse or asymmetric C2. Check whether the destination IP is a known reflection/amplification service (DNS, NTP, memcached) or an unusual host. Correlate with SYN/RST counts to rule out scanning artifacts producing the skewed ratio.

rules.description: Detects a long-duration flow using a destination port outside the well-known service range, which can indicate command-and-control traffic deliberately using a non-standard or high ephemeral port to blend in with normal outbound traffic and evade port-based firewall rules.
rules.recommendation: Verify whether the destination port is associated with any known legitimate application on the source host (e.g. an ephemeral client-side port reused for a known service) before treating as suspicious. Check for repeated connections to the same non-standard port across multiple sessions or hosts, which would strengthen the C2 hypothesis. Capture traffic content for protocol fingerprinting.

rules.description: Detects a flow with an extremely high packet rate combined with a high byte rate, exceeding normal application traffic patterns. This composite indicator flags flows that are both fast and voluminous, which merit review as either a flood/DoS technique or an unusually aggressive bulk transfer.
rules.recommendation: Determine whether the flow's rate profile matches a known legitimate high-throughput service (backup job, media streaming) before escalating. If unexplained, check for coincident CPU/bandwidth exhaustion on the destination host and correlate with other flows from the same source in the same window to detect a broader flood pattern.
